---
title: "1.3 Statistical Significance of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: false
include-after-body:
  - ../../../../styles/web-note-modal.html
---

::: {.section-output-links}
[Slides](video-lesson-slides.qmd){.action-video aria-label="Open video lesson slides" data-modal="video" data-label="Video lesson slides" data-tooltip="Slides"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[PDF](pdf-notes.qmd){.action-pdf aria-label="Open PDF notes" data-tooltip="PDF"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Markdown](downloads/section.json){.action-markdown aria-label="Open raw markdown viewer" data-modal="markdown" data-label="Markdown" data-tooltip="Markdown" data-markdown-src="downloads/section.json"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Bundle](downloads/section-bundle.zip){.action-bundle aria-label="Download markdown bundle" data-tooltip="Bundle"}
:::

# A Good Backtest Still Has a Finite-Sample Problem

Even when a backtest is carefully implemented, its summary statistics are based
on a limited historical sample. Average return, Sharpe ratio, and drawdown are
all random variables in that sense. A profitable backtest may indicate a real
edge, but it may also reflect a favorable historical sample that happened to
reward the strategy.

Chan frames this as a statistical-significance problem. The practical question
is not only whether the backtest made money, but whether the observed result is
surprising enough that pure luck becomes an implausible explanation.

# Hypothesis Testing Starts from a Skeptical Null

The standard framework begins with a null hypothesis. In this context, the null
hypothesis says that the strategy's true average daily return, over an
effectively infinite sample, is zero. The backtest then produces a test
statistic from the observed sample. For a simple example, that statistic could
be the average daily return itself.

The next step is to specify the probability distribution implied by the null
hypothesis. Once that distribution is fixed, we ask how likely it would be to
see a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed in the backtest.
That probability is the p-value.

If the p-value is very small, the null hypothesis becomes difficult to keep. A
common threshold is 0.01. Reaching such a threshold does not prove that the
strategy will keep working, but it does support the claim that the historical
result is not easily explained as chance under the chosen null model.

# Sharpe Ratio Connects Naturally to Significance

Under a Gaussian assumption, the test becomes especially intuitive. The
relevant statistic is proportional to the daily Sharpe ratio multiplied by the
square root of the number of observations:

$$
t \approx \text{Sharpe}_{\text{daily}} \sqrt{n}
$$

This matches trading intuition. A strategy looks more statistically persuasive
when it has either a stronger signal per unit of risk or a longer history over
which that signal has repeated. One spectacular short sample is usually less
convincing than many observations of a moderate but persistent edge.

Chan reproduces critical values that convert this statistic into p-value
thresholds:

| p-value threshold | Critical value for daily Sharpe x $\sqrt{n}$ |
| ----------------: | -------------------------------------------: |
| 0.10              | 1.282                                        |
| 0.05              | 1.645                                        |
| 0.01              | 2.326                                        |
| 0.001             | 3.091                                        |

These cutoffs give a simple reading rule. For example, if the daily Sharpe
ratio times $\sqrt{n}$ is at least 2.326, then the p-value is at most 0.01
under the Gaussian null.

# Choosing the Null Distribution Is the Real Modeling Decision

Chan emphasizes that the most delicate step is not calculating the p-value once
a null distribution is known. The difficult part is deciding what that null
distribution should be.

One option is parametric. We assume daily returns follow a Gaussian
distribution with mean zero under the null and with standard deviation
estimated from the sample. This choice is simple and produces an immediate link
to the Sharpe ratio.

Another option is simulation-based. The researcher can generate many synthetic
historical price series under a null market model and rerun the trading
strategy on each of them. The p-value is then estimated empirically as the
fraction of simulated histories that produce an average return at least as
large as the observed backtest return.

The appeal of Monte Carlo testing is conceptual. A trading strategy may depend
on subtle path structure, timing, or correlation patterns that are not captured
well by only the first few moments of returns. Running the actual strategy over
many simulated histories can be a more direct way to ask whether the observed
profit is unusual.

# Statistical Significance Does Not Replace Market Realism

A statistically significant backtest is stronger than an impressive but
unexamined equity curve. It shows that, under the chosen null model, the
observed result is hard to dismiss as random luck. But it still does not answer
every practical question.

Significance does not fix look-ahead bias, survivorship bias, incorrect auction
prices, missing borrow constraints, or unrealistic venue assumptions. It does
not guarantee that transaction costs are modeled correctly. It does not prove
that market structure will remain stable in the future.

The correct interpretation is narrower and more useful. Statistical
significance is one gate in the evaluation process. After a backtest passes the
market-reality checks from the previous section, hypothesis testing helps judge
whether the remaining performance evidence is strong enough to take seriously.

# Review Checklist

- What is the test statistic in this backtest: average return, Sharpe ratio, or
  something else?
- What null hypothesis is being tested?
- Which null distribution is assumed: a parametric model or a simulation-based
  model?
- Is the sample large enough for the claimed significance to be persuasive?
- Does the strategy remain realistic after costs, timing, venue, and execution
  constraints are considered?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting](../02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)
- Next: [1.4 When Not to Backtest a Strategy](../04-when-not-to-backtest-a-strategy/web-notes.qmd)
